solution:
Solution. The identity
\[
\sin 3 \theta-\sin \theta=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}(3 \theta-\theta) \cos \frac{1}{2}(3 \theta+\theta)
\]
leads to the identity
\[
\sin 3 \theta=\sin \theta(1+2 \cos 2 \theta) .
\]

Hence for any \( \boldsymbol{x} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin x & =\sin (x / 3)(1+2 \cos (2 x / 3)) \\
& =\sin (x / 9)(1+2 \cos (2 x / 9))(1+2 \cos (2 x / 3))
\end{aligned}
\]
and after \( \boldsymbol{n} \) iterations we have
\[
\sin x=\sin \left(x / 3^{n}\right) \prod_{k=1}^{n}\left(1+2 \cos \left(2 x / 3^{k}\right)\right)
\]

For \( x=0 \), the right member of the required relation is not defined. However, if we interpret \( (\sin 0) / 0 \) as \( 1\left(=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \sin x / x\right) \), then the required equation is correct since every factor in the infinite product is 1.

If \( x \neq 0 \), we divide (1) by \( x \) and rearrange to get
\[
\frac{\sin x}{x}=\frac{\sin \left(x / 3^{n}\right)}{x / 3^{n}} \prod_{k=1}^{n}\left(\frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 x / 3^{k}\right)}{3}\right) .
\]

Now
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(x / 3^{n}\right)}{x / 3^{n}}=1,
\]
and it follows that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 x / 3^{k}\right)}{3}=\frac{\sin x}{x} .
\]

Remark. Because of the special behavior of 0 in multiplication, the convergence of infinite products is not usually decided simply by the convergence of the sequence of partial products. Rather, it is required that only finitely many factors be zero and that the partial products of the others have a non-zero limit. (See, for example, Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill, New York, 1966, p. 189.) The infinite product above converges in this restricted sense because for any fixed \( \boldsymbol{x} \),
\[
1+2 \cos \frac{2 x}{3^{k}}
\]
does not vanish for large \( k \) and the above proof shows that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{k=t+1}^{n}\left(1+2 \cos \frac{2 x}{3^{k}}\right)=\frac{\sin \left(x / 3^{t}\right)}{x / 3^{t}} \neq 0
\]
for large \( t \).

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The identity
\[
\sin 3 anglevar-\sin anglevar=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}(3 anglevar-anglevar) \cos \frac{1}{2}(3 anglevar+anglevar)
\]
leads to the identity
\[
\sin 3 anglevar=\sin anglevar(1+2 \cos 2 anglevar) .
\]

Hence for any \( \boldsymbol{inputvar} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin inputvar & =\sin (inputvar / 3)(1+2 \cos (2 inputvar / 3)) \\
& =\sin (inputvar / 9)(1+2 \cos (2 inputvar / 9))(1+2 \cos (2 inputvar / 3))
\end{aligned}
\]
and after \( \boldsymbol{levelnum} \) iterations we have
\[
\sin inputvar=\sin \left(inputvar / 3^{levelnum}\right) \prod_{termindex=1}^{levelnum}\left(1+2 \cos \left(2 inputvar / 3^{termindex}\right)\right)
\]

For \( inputvar=0 \), the right member of the required relation is not defined. However, if we interpret \( (\sin 0) / 0 \) as \( 1\left(=\lim _{inputvar \rightarrow 0} \sin inputvar / inputvar\right) \), then the required equation is correct since every factor in the infinite product is 1.

If \( inputvar \neq 0 \), we divide (1) by \( inputvar \) and rearrange to get
\[
\frac{\sin inputvar}{inputvar}=\frac{\sin \left(inputvar / 3^{levelnum}\right)}{inputvar / 3^{levelnum}} \prod_{termindex=1}^{levelnum}\left(\frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 inputvar / 3^{termindex}\right)}{3}\right) .
\]

Now
\[
\lim _{levelnum \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(inputvar / 3^{levelnum}\right)}{inputvar / 3^{levelnum}}=1,
\]
and it follows that
\[
\lim _{levelnum \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{termindex=1}^{levelnum} \frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 inputvar / 3^{termindex}\right)}{3}=\frac{\sin inputvar}{inputvar} .
\]

Remark. Because of the special behavior of 0 in multiplication, the convergence of infinite products is not usually decided simply by the convergence of the sequence of partial products. Rather, it is required that only finitely many factors be zero and that the partial products of the others have a non-zero limit. (See, for example, Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill, New York, 1966, p. 189.) The infinite product above converges in this restricted sense because for any fixed \( \boldsymbol{inputvar} \),
\[
1+2 \cos \frac{2 inputvar}{3^{termindex}}
\]
does not vanish for large \( termindex \) and the above proof shows that
\[
\lim _{levelnum \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{termindex=shiftidx+1}^{levelnum}\left(1+2 \cos \frac{2 inputvar}{3^{termindex}}\right)=\frac{\sin \left(inputvar / 3^{shiftidx}\right)}{inputvar / 3^{shiftidx}} \neq 0
\]
for large \( shiftidx \).

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The identity
\[
\sin 3 butterfly-\sin butterfly=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}(3 butterfly-butterfly) \cos \frac{1}{2}(3 butterfly+butterfly)
\]
leads to the identity
\[
\sin 3 butterfly=\sin butterfly(1+2 \cos 2 butterfly) .
\]

Hence for any \( \boldsymbol{mapleleaf} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin mapleleaf & =\sin (mapleleaf / 3)(1+2 \cos (2 mapleleaf / 3)) \\
& =\sin (mapleleaf / 9)(1+2 \cos (2 mapleleaf / 9))(1+2 \cos (2 mapleleaf / 3))
\end{aligned}
\]
and after \( \boldsymbol{sandcastle} \) iterations we have
\[
\sin mapleleaf=\sin \left(mapleleaf / 3^{sandcastle}\right) \prod_{snowflake=1}^{sandcastle}\left(1+2 \cos \left(2 mapleleaf / 3^{snowflake}\right)\right)
\]

For \( mapleleaf=0 \), the right member of the required relation is not defined. However, if we interpret \( (\sin 0) / 0 \) as \( 1\left(=\lim _{mapleleaf \rightarrow 0} \sin mapleleaf / mapleleaf\right) \), then the required equation is correct since every factor in the infinite product is 1.

If \( mapleleaf \neq 0 \), we divide (1) by \( mapleleaf \) and rearrange to get
\[
\frac{\sin mapleleaf}{mapleleaf}=\frac{\sin \left(mapleleaf / 3^{sandcastle}\right)}{mapleleaf / 3^{sandcastle}} \prod_{snowflake=1}^{sandcastle}\left(\frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 mapleleaf / 3^{snowflake}\right)}{3}\right) .
\]

Now
\[
\lim _{sandcastle \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(mapleleaf / 3^{sandcastle}\right)}{mapleleaf / 3^{sandcastle}}=1,
\]
and it follows that
\[
\lim _{sandcastle \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{snowflake=1}^{sandcastle} \frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 mapleleaf / 3^{snowflake}\right)}{3}=\frac{\sin mapleleaf}{mapleleaf} .
\]

Remark. Because of the special behavior of 0 in multiplication, the convergence of infinite products is not usually decided simply by the convergence of the sequence of partial products. Rather, it is required that only finitely many factors be zero and that the partial products of the others have a non-zero limit. (See, for example, Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill, New York, 1966, p. 189.) The infinite product above converges in this restricted sense because for any fixed \( \boldsymbol{mapleleaf} \),
\[
1+2 \cos \frac{2 mapleleaf}{3^{snowflake}}
\]
does not vanish for large \( snowflake \) and the above proof shows that
\[
\lim _{sandcastle \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{snowflake=moonlight+1}^{sandcastle}\left(1+2 \cos \frac{2 mapleleaf}{3^{snowflake}}\right)=\frac{\sin \left(mapleleaf / 3^{moonlight}\right)}{mapleleaf / 3^{moonlight}} \neq 0
\]
for large \( moonlight \).

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The identity
\[
\sin 3 straightline-\sin straightline=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}(3 straightline-straightline) \cos \frac{1}{2}(3 straightline+straightline)
\]
leads to the identity
\[
\sin 3 straightline=\sin straightline(1+2 \cos 2 straightline) .
\]

Hence for any \( \boldsymbol{constantval} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin constantval & =\sin (constantval / 3)(1+2 \cos (2 constantval / 3)) \\
& =\sin (constantval / 9)(1+2 \cos (2 constantval / 9))(1+2 \cos (2 constantval / 3))
\end{aligned}
\]
and after \( \boldsymbol{continuous} \) iterations we have
\[
\sin constantval=\sin \left(constantval / 3^{continuous}\right) \prod_{unindexed=1}^{continuous}\left(1+2 \cos \left(2 constantval / 3^{unindexed}\right)\right)
\]

For \( constantval=0 \), the right member of the required relation is not defined. However, if we interpret \( (\sin 0) / 0 \) as \( 1\left(=\lim _{constantval \rightarrow 0} \sin constantval / constantval\right) \), then the required equation is correct since every factor in the infinite product is 1.

If \( constantval \neq 0 \), we divide (1) by \( constantval \) and rearrange to get
\[
\frac{\sin constantval}{constantval}=\frac{\sin \left(constantval / 3^{continuous}\right)}{constantval / 3^{continuous}} \prod_{unindexed=1}^{continuous}\left(\frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 constantval / 3^{unindexed}\right)}{3}\right) .
\]

Now
\[
\lim _{continuous \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(constantval / 3^{continuous}\right)}{constantval / 3^{continuous}}=1,
\]
and it follows that
\[
\lim _{continuous \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{unindexed=1}^{continuous} \frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 constantval / 3^{unindexed}\right)}{3}=\frac{\sin constantval}{constantval} .
\]

Remark. Because of the special behavior of 0 in multiplication, the convergence of infinite products is not usually decided simply by the convergence of the sequence of partial products. Rather, it is required that only finitely many factors be zero and that the partial products of the others have a non-zero limit. (See, for example, Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill, New York, 1966, p. 189.) The infinite product above converges in this restricted sense because for any fixed \( \boldsymbol{constantval} \),
\[
1+2 \cos \frac{2 constantval}{3^{unindexed}}
\]
does not vanish for large \( unindexed \) and the above proof shows that
\[
\lim _{continuous \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{unindexed=finality+1}^{continuous}\left(1+2 \cos \frac{2 constantval}{3^{unindexed}}\right)=\frac{\sin \left(constantval / 3^{finality}\right)}{constantval / 3^{finality}} \neq 0
\]
for large \( finality \).

garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The identity
\[
\sin 3 mnqrdskj-\sin mnqrdskj=2 \sin \frac{1}{2}(3 mnqrdskj-mnqrdskj) \cos \frac{1}{2}(3 mnqrdskj+mnqrdskj)
\]
leads to the identity
\[
\sin 3 mnqrdskj=\sin mnqrdskj(1+2 \cos 2 mnqrdskj) .
\]

Hence for any \( \boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp} \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sin qzxwvtnp & =\sin (qzxwvtnp / 3)(1+2 \cos (2 qzxwvtnp / 3)) \\
& =\sin (qzxwvtnp / 9)(1+2 \cos (2 qzxwvtnp / 9))(1+2 \cos (2 qzxwvtnp / 3))
\end{aligned}
\]
and after \( \boldsymbol{vbmshqtz} \) iterations we have
\[
\sin qzxwvtnp=\sin \left(qzxwvtnp / 3^{vbmshqtz}\right) \prod_{hjgrksla=1}^{vbmshqtz}\left(1+2 \cos \left(2 qzxwvtnp / 3^{hjgrksla}\right)\right)
\]

For \( qzxwvtnp=0 \), the right member of the required relation is not defined. However, if we interpret \( (\sin 0) / 0 \) as \( 1\left(=\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow 0} \sin qzxwvtnp / qzxwvtnp\right) \), then the required equation is correct since every factor in the infinite product is 1.

If \( qzxwvtnp \neq 0 \), we divide (1) by \( qzxwvtnp \) and rearrange to get
\[
\frac{\sin qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}=\frac{\sin \left(qzxwvtnp / 3^{vbmshqtz}\right)}{qzxwvtnp / 3^{vbmshqtz}} \prod_{hjgrksla=1}^{vbmshqtz}\left(\frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 qzxwvtnp / 3^{hjgrksla}\right)}{3}\right) .
\]

Now
\[
\lim _{vbmshqtz \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\sin \left(qzxwvtnp / 3^{vbmshqtz}\right)}{qzxwvtnp / 3^{vbmshqtz}}=1,
\]
and it follows that
\[
\lim _{vbmshqtz \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{hjgrksla=1}^{vbmshqtz} \frac{1+2 \cos \left(2 qzxwvtnp / 3^{hjgrksla}\right)}{3}=\frac{\sin qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp} .
\]

Remark. Because of the special behavior of 0 in multiplication, the convergence of infinite products is not usually decided simply by the convergence of the sequence of partial products. Rather, it is required that only finitely many factors be zero and that the partial products of the others have a non-zero limit. (See, for example, Ahlfors, Complex Analysis, 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill, New York, 1966, p. 189.) The infinite product above converges in this restricted sense because for any fixed \( \boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp} \),
\[
1+2 \cos \frac{2 qzxwvtnp}{3^{hjgrksla}}
\]
does not vanish for large \( hjgrksla \) and the above proof shows that
\[
\lim _{vbmshqtz \rightarrow \infty} \prod_{hjgrksla=xzcflgpo+1}^{vbmshqtz}\left(1+2 \cos \frac{2 qzxwvtnp}{3^{hjgrksla}}\right)=\frac{\sin \left(qzxwvtnp / 3^{xzcflgpo}\right)}{qzxwvtnp / 3^{xzcflgpo}} \neq 0
\]
for large \( xzcflgpo \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer and denote by $U_{m-1}$ the $(m-1)$-st Chebyshev polynomial of the second kind, i.e.  
\[
U_{m-1}(\cos\theta)=\frac{\sin (m\theta)}{\sin\theta},\qquad\theta\in\mathbf R .
\]

1.  Prove the infinite-product identity  
\[
\forall z\in\mathbf C\qquad 
\prod_{k=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}\bigl(\cos(z/m^{\,k+1})\bigr)\Bigr)=\frac{\sin z}{z}.
\tag{$\star$}
\]

2.  Show that the product in $(\star)$ converges absolutely and locally uniformly on $\mathbf C$; hence the left-hand side defines an entire function.

3.  By taking logarithmic derivatives of the factors in $(\star)$ and integrating, derive Euler's classical Weierstrass factorisation  
\[
\sin z = z\prod_{n=1}^{\infty}\Bigl(1-\frac{z^{2}}{\pi^{2}n^{2}}\Bigr).
\tag{$\heartsuit$}
\]

4.  Finally, prove that $\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$ is the unique entire function of order $1$ whose non-zero zeros are precisely the integer multiples of $\pi$ and which satisfies $(\star)$.  
(As usual, $\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$ is understood at $z=0$ by continuity.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  
\[
f_m(z):=\prod_{k=0}^{\infty}\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}\bigl(\cos(z/m^{\,k+1})\bigr)\Bigr),\qquad z\in\mathbf C .
\tag{0}
\]

Step 1.  A one-step decomposition of $\sin$.  
Because $U_{m-1}(\cos\theta)=\dfrac{\sin (m\theta)}{\sin\theta}$ we have  
\[
\sin (m\theta)=\sin\theta\;U_{m-1}(\cos\theta).
\tag{1}
\]
Putting $\theta=\dfrac{z}{m}$ gives  
\[
\sin z=\sin\!\bigl(z/m\bigr)\,U_{m-1}\!\bigl(\cos(z/m)\bigr).
\tag{2}
\]

Step 2.  Finite iteration.  
Iterating $(2)$ $N$ times yields  
\[
\sin z=\sin\!\bigl(z/m^{N}\bigr)\prod_{k=1}^{N}U_{m-1}\!\bigl(\cos(z/m^{k})\bigr).
\tag{3}
\]
Dividing by $z$ and extracting powers of $m$ we obtain  
\[
\frac{\sin z}{z}=\frac{\sin\!\bigl(z/m^{N}\bigr)}{z/m^{N}}
              \prod_{k=1}^{N}\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}\bigl(\cos(z/m^{k})\bigr)\Bigr).
\tag{4}
\]

Step 3.  Absolute and locally uniform convergence (problem 2).  
For small $t$  
\[
\cos t=1-\frac{t^{2}}{2}+O(t^{4}),\qquad
U_{m-1}(\cos t)=m-\frac{m(m^{2}-1)}{6}\,t^{2}+O(t^{4}).
\]
Hence  
\[
\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}(\cos t)=1-\frac{(m^{2}-1)t^{2}}{6}+O(t^{4}).
\tag{5}
\]
Put $t=z/m^{k+1}$.  On the disc $\lvert z\rvert\le R$ one has  
\[
\bigl\lvert\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}(\cos(z/m^{k+1}))-1\bigr\rvert\le C_{R}\,m^{-2k-2},
\]
so $\sum_{k\ge 0}\lvert\log\text{factor}_{k}\rvert$ converges absolutely and uniformly on $\lvert z\rvert\le R$ (because $\sum_{k}m^{-2k}<\infty$).  Therefore the product in $(0)$ converges absolutely and locally uniformly on $\mathbf C$ and $f_m$ is entire.

Step 4.  Passage to the limit $N\to\infty$ (problem 1).  
Since $\displaystyle\lim_{w\to 0}\dfrac{\sin w}{w}=1$, letting $N\to\infty$ in $(4)$ and using the uniform convergence just proved we get  
\[
f_m(z)=\frac{\sin z}{z},\qquad z\in\mathbf C .
\tag{6}
\]

Step 5.  Logarithmic derivatives and Euler's product (problem 3).

5.1  Derivative of a single factor.  
For $t=z/m^{k+1}$ set $g(t)=\log\bigl(\dfrac{\sin(mt)}{m\sin t}\bigr)$.  Then
\[
g'(t)=m\cot(mt)-\cot t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm d z}\log\!\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}(\cos(z/m^{k+1}))\Bigr)
          =\frac1{m^{k}}\cot\!\bigl(z/m^{k}\bigr)
           -\frac1{m^{k+1}}\cot\!\bigl(z/m^{k+1}\bigr).
\tag{7}
\]

5.2  Telescoping in $k$.  
Summing $(7)$ from $k=0$ to $N$ we obtain  
\[
S_N(z):=\sum_{k=0}^{N}\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dz}\log\!\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}(\cos(z/m^{k+1}))\Bigr)
      =\cot z-\frac1{m^{N+1}}\cot\!\Bigl(\tfrac{z}{m^{N+1}}\Bigr).
\tag{8}
\]
As $w\to 0$, $\cot w=\dfrac1w-\dfrac w3+O(w^{3})$, whence  
\[
\frac1{m^{N+1}}\cot\!\Bigl(\tfrac{z}{m^{N+1}}\Bigr)
      =\frac1z-\frac{z}{3m^{2N+2}}+O(m^{-4N-4})\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}\frac1z .
\]
Hence
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}S_N(z)=\cot z-\frac1z .
\tag{9}
\]

5.3  Agreement with the right-hand side.  
Because of $(6)$, $f_m(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$, whose logarithmic derivative is precisely $\cot z-\dfrac1z$.  The limit in $(9)$ therefore equals $\dfrac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dz}\log f_m(z)$, as required.

5.4  Partial-fraction expansion.  
The meromorphic function $\cot z-\dfrac1z$ has simple poles at $z=\pm\pi n$ $(n\in\mathbf Z\setminus\{0\})$ with residue $+1$.  Its classical expansion is  
\[
\cot z-\frac1z=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2z}{z^{2}-\pi^{2}n^{2}},\qquad z\notin\pi\mathbf Z,
\tag{10}
\]
where the series converges absolutely and locally uniformly because $\sum_{n\ge 1}n^{-2}<\infty$.

5.5  Integration.  
Integrating $(10)$ from $0$ to $z$ along any path avoiding $\pi\mathbf Z$ we get  
\[
\log(\sin z)-\log z=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\log\Bigl(1-\frac{z^{2}}{\pi^{2}n^{2}}\Bigr).
\]
Since both sides vanish at $z=0$, no additive constant appears.  Exponentiating yields Euler's factorisation $(\heartsuit)$.

Step 6.  Uniqueness (problem 4).  
Let $g$ be an entire function of order $1$ whose non-zero zeros are the simple zeros $\pi n$, $n\in\mathbf Z\setminus\{0\}$, and which satisfies $(\star)$.  Then $g$ and $\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$ share the same zeros and the same canonical product (the left-hand side of $(\star)$).  Consequently
\[
h(z):=\frac{g(z)}{\sin z/z}
\]
is an entire function without zeros.  Moreover $h(0)=1$ (limit value) and $h$ is of order at most $1$.  By standard Hadamard factorisation, an entire function of finite order with no zeros must be of the form $e^{az+b}$; the condition $h(0)=1$ forces $b=0$.  Inserting this into $(\star)$ we get $h(z)\equiv 1$ (otherwise $(\star)$ fails at $z=0$).  Hence $g(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$, completing the proof of uniqueness.

\[
\boxed{\;f_m(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z}\;}
\qquad\text{for all }z\in\mathbf C.\quad\blacksquare
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher algebraic structure: the problem replaces the elementary cosine factor by a Chebyshev polynomial Uₘ₋₁, demanding familiarity with special polynomials and the identity sin(m θ)=sin θ Uₘ₋₁(cos θ).

• Additional parameters: the integer m ≥ 2 introduces a family of identities; handling an arbitrary m requires uniform estimates and careful parameter tracking.

• Deeper analytic requirements: the proof now needs absolute and locally uniform convergence of an infinite product of non-trivial analytic factors, invoking Taylor expansions and uniform majorants.

• Entire-function theory: deriving the Weierstrass product and proving uniqueness obliges the solver to use Hadamard’s factorisation theorem and growth–order arguments—well beyond the techniques needed for the original cosine product.

• Multi-layer reasoning: the solution combines trigonometric identities, polynomial theory, complex analysis of infinite products, and entire-function uniqueness, providing substantially more conceptual and technical depth than either the original problem or the simpler “cos(z/2^{k})” kernel variant.

g'(t)=m\cot(mt)-\cot t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm d z}\log\!\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}(\cos(z/m^{k+1}))\Bigr)
          =\frac1{m^{k}}\cot\!\bigl(z/m^{k}\bigr)
           -\frac1{m^{k+1}}\cot\!\bigl(z/m^{k+1}\bigr).
\tag{7}
\]

5.2  Telescoping in $k$.  
Summing $(7)$ from $k=0$ to $N$ we obtain  
\[
S_N(z):=\sum_{k=0}^{N}\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dz}\log\!\Bigl(\tfrac1m\,U_{m-1}(\cos(z/m^{k+1}))\Bigr)
      =\cot z-\frac1{m^{N+1}}\cot\!\Bigl(\tfrac{z}{m^{N+1}}\Bigr).
\tag{8}
\]
As $w\to 0$, $\cot w=\dfrac1w-\dfrac w3+O(w^{3})$, whence  
\[
\frac1{m^{N+1}}\cot\!\Bigl(\tfrac{z}{m^{N+1}}\Bigr)
      =\frac1z-\frac{z}{3m^{2N+2}}+O(m^{-4N-4})\xrightarrow[N\to\infty]{}\frac1z .
\]
Hence
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}S_N(z)=\cot z-\frac1z .
\tag{9}
\]

5.3  Agreement with the right-hand side.  
Because of $(6)$, $f_m(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$, whose logarithmic derivative is precisely $\cot z-\dfrac1z$.  The limit in $(9)$ therefore equals $\dfrac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dz}\log f_m(z)$, as required.

5.4  Partial-fraction expansion.  
The meromorphic function $\cot z-\dfrac1z$ has simple poles at $z=\pm\pi n$ $(n\in\mathbf Z\setminus\{0\})$ with residue $+1$.  Its classical expansion is  
\[
\cot z-\frac1z=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{2z}{z^{2}-\pi^{2}n^{2}},\qquad z\notin\pi\mathbf Z,
\tag{10}
\]
where the series converges absolutely and locally uniformly because $\sum_{n\ge 1}n^{-2}<\infty$.

5.5  Integration.  
Integrating $(10)$ from $0$ to $z$ along any path avoiding $\pi\mathbf Z$ we get  
\[
\log(\sin z)-\log z=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\log\Bigl(1-\frac{z^{2}}{\pi^{2}n^{2}}\Bigr).
\]
Since both sides vanish at $z=0$, no additive constant appears.  Exponentiating yields Euler's factorisation $(\heartsuit)$.

Step 6.  Uniqueness (problem 4).  
Let $g$ be an entire function of order $1$ whose non-zero zeros are the simple zeros $\pi n$, $n\in\mathbf Z\setminus\{0\}$, and which satisfies $(\star)$.  Then $g$ and $\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$ share the same zeros and the same canonical product (the left-hand side of $(\star)$).  Consequently
\[
h(z):=\frac{g(z)}{\sin z/z}
\]
is an entire function without zeros.  Moreover $h(0)=1$ (limit value) and $h$ is of order at most $1$.  By standard Hadamard factorisation, an entire function of finite order with no zeros must be of the form $e^{az+b}$; the condition $h(0)=1$ forces $b=0$.  Inserting this into $(\star)$ we get $h(z)\equiv 1$ (otherwise $(\star)$ fails at $z=0$).  Hence $g(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z}$, completing the proof of uniqueness.

\[
\boxed{\;f_m(z)=\dfrac{\sin z}{z}\;}
\qquad\text{for all }z\in\mathbf C.\quad\blacksquare
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher algebraic structure: the problem replaces the elementary cosine factor by a Chebyshev polynomial Uₘ₋₁, demanding familiarity with special polynomials and the identity sin(m θ)=sin θ Uₘ₋₁(cos θ).

• Additional parameters: the integer m ≥ 2 introduces a family of identities; handling an arbitrary m requires uniform estimates and careful parameter tracking.

• Deeper analytic requirements: the proof now needs absolute and locally uniform convergence of an infinite product of non-trivial analytic factors, invoking Taylor expansions and uniform majorants.

• Entire-function theory: deriving the Weierstrass product and proving uniqueness obliges the solver to use Hadamard’s factorisation theorem and growth–order arguments—well beyond the techniques needed for the original cosine product.

• Multi-layer reasoning: the solution combines trigonometric identities, polynomial theory, complex analysis of infinite products, and entire-function uniqueness, providing substantially more conceptual and technical depth than either the original problem or the simpler “cos(z/2^{k})” kernel variant.
